main_summary.summary: Robert G. Clark Jr., the first African American elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives in the twentieth century, was interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer on March 14, 2013, at Clark's family home in Pickens, Holmes County, Mississippi. Clark recounts being born in the house his father built on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved and which the family acquired after emancipation. He describes walking miles to a four-to-five-month grammar school, attending Holmes County Training School (now Williams-Sullivan School) at Durant, and working his way through Jackson College under President Jacob L. Reddix and Dean H.T. Sampson. After teaching at Humphreys County Training School near Louise, Mississippi, he was dismissed for supporting school integration; his father was likewise fired and blacklisted via the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission. Clark earned a master's degree at Michigan State University, returned to Holmes County during Freedom Summer, entered the movement through Henry and Sue Lorenzi, and won a 1967 legislative seat as an independent. He details Marian Wright's defense of his seating, his alliance with Buddie Newman, his chairmanship of the House Education Committee, the passage of Governor William Winter's 1982 Education Reform Act restoring compulsory attendance and adding kindergarten, his two losing campaigns against Webb Franklin for the U.S. House, and his 1992 election as Speaker Pro Tempore.
key_themes: Family land ownership from emancipation through twentieth century
Black educators fired for supporting integration and voter registration
Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission surveillance and blacklisting
Independent legislative election under the post-Voting-Rights-Act franchise
Mississippi Education Reform Act of 1982 and compulsory school attendance
main_summary.historical_significance:
Clark is the sole primary-source witness to the political opening of the Mississippi House of Representatives to Black members after Reconstruction. His account documents the mechanisms of the Sovereignty Commission's school-employment blacklist (operating against both him and his father), the procedural isolation tactics deployed against him during his first eight years as the only Black legislator, and the legislative maneuvering that produced the 1982 Education Reform Act with Governor William Winter. The interview also preserves first-hand testimony on the 1955 Brown-era firings of Black teachers in the Mississippi Delta, the Belzoni shooting of a voter-registration activist Clark places at his own barbershop chair, and the Holmes County Civil Rights Movement's connection to the Lorenzis and Freedom Summer.

As a Black officeholder who served continuously from 1967 through the era of his 1992 Speaker Pro Tempore election, Clark provides a continuous narrative bridging the pre-Voting-Rights-Act educational world of his own childhood (segregated four-month schools, his father acquitted of murder in 1940) and the post-Voting-Rights-Act legislative environment. His account of two campaigns against Webb Franklin documents how racial-coded electoral appeals ("Webb Franklin, he's one of us") operated in 1980s Mississippi congressional contests.

chapters.title: Family Land and Birthplace
chapters.summary: Clark describes being born in the house his father built approximately one hundred years before the interview, on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved. After emancipation his great-grandfather's former owner sold him the property; Clark's father later urged him to return after he finished twelfth grade, and Clark agreed despite wanting to leave for Chicago. The interview is conducted in that same house.
related_events: Emancipation land transfer in Holmes County
Clark's return to family homestead after high school

chapters.title: 1940 Arrest at Age Eleven
chapters.summary: Clark and his brother were jailed overnight with their father, who had been arrested on murder charges after a man was found dead about two miles from the family property. A white lawyer in Lexington defended the father and won acquittal on self-defense grounds. Clark says he was twelve at the time and believes the jailing of the children was intimidation.
related_events: 1940 Holmes County murder trial of Clark's father

chapters.title: Growing Up on Family Land
chapters.summary: Because his family owned 228 acres and never sharecropped, Clark says he never worked in a white man's cotton field, and other Black families regarded the Clarks as different. He attended a two-teacher grammar school three miles away that ran four to five months a year so children could be released to chop and pick cotton; white school buses passed throwing spitballs.
related_events: Holmes County segregated rural schooling

chapters.title: High School and Segregation
chapters.summary: Clark traveled sixteen miles to Lexington and twenty-four to Durant for high school, boarding because there were no buses for African American students; he then walked from his porch to Pickens to catch the 2 a.m. train to school. His mother and grandfather, who had been enslaved in Hinds County and was eleven at emancipation, taught Clark that he was equal to whites but had to observe Jim Crow rules.
related_events: Clark's enrollment at Holmes County Training School at Durant

chapters.title: Jackson College and the Track Scholarship
chapters.summary: Clark wanted to attend Tougaloo to study pre-law and become a lawyer like Perry W. Howard, but Tougaloo told him a work-aid student would take eight years to graduate. He walked to Pickens, took the Trailways bus to Jackson with less than five dollars, and was kept overnight at Jackson College (then Jackson College for Negro Teachers); Dr. Jacob L. Reddix arranged custodial and dormitory garbage work at twenty-five cents an hour to cover tuition. Clark became the first track scholarship recipient at what is now Jackson State University after the Southwestern Athletic Conference championship.
related_events: Clark's first-year enrollment at Jackson College for Negro Teachers

chapters.title: Dropping Out and Becoming a Teacher
chapters.summary: A dean told Clark Jackson State would not let him use teaching to become a lawyer and ordered him to leave if he wanted law school; without anyone to tell him he could practice law without a pre-law major, he stayed in teaching. When Dean H.T. Sampson would not implement a one-student health-and-physical-education program, Clark left at the end of fall quarter to teach at Williams-Sullivan rather than spend a fifth year. He later returned for his degree in 1952.
related_events: Clark's first teaching post at Williams-Sullivan School

chapters.title: Fired in Louise, Mississippi
chapters.summary: Teaching at Louise, Mississippi during the Brown v. Board era, Clark told the superintendent, Mr. Brown, that white and Black children should attend school together; at the end of the year he was told he was no longer needed. Emmett Till was killed soon after at the 1955 Southwestern Athletic Conference workshop. A barbershop visit in Belzoni, Mississippi placed Clark in the chair just before Rubin Lee (whom Clark identifies as a voter-registration activist) was shot dead about two hours later.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education backlash firings
1955 Emmett Till murder
Belzoni voter-registration shooting

chapters.title: Sovereignty Commission and Father's Firing
chapters.summary: Clark describes the Citizens' Councils as ruling Mississippi above the legislature and governor during the Brown backlash, and recounts seeing his name in a Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission report in which the Humphreys County superintendent claimed he had "gotten rid of all of the troublemakers." His father was fired in Humphreys County for teaching voter registration classes, took a Rosenwald school principalship in Madison County, was fired there, and could not get hired again because the Sovereignty Commission circulated names to all superintendents. Clark says the Commission used Black informants.
related_events: Sovereignty Commission blacklisting of Black teachers
Citizens' Council formation after Brown decision

chapters.title: Michigan State University
chapters.summary: Mississippi had no graduate school for African Americans, so the state paid the out-of-state differential for Clark to attend Michigan State University. He chose Michigan State over Notre Dame ("taught too much religion") and the University of California (cost of travel). In a class of thirty-five he was the only African American; after two students answered a question wrong, Clark answered correctly and says he never feared being among white students again, which prepared him for the legislature.
related_events: Mississippi's out-of-state Black-graduate-school tuition policy

chapters.title: Entering the Civil Rights Movement
chapters.summary: Teaching and coaching at Lexington High School from 1961 to 1966, Clark says he initially knew little of the movement and that some Black residents suspected the white community had sent him to spy. He entered the movement through Henry and Sue Lorenzi, the white couple who arrived in Holmes County in fall 1964. About thirty Freedom Summer volunteers worked in Holmes County, and some of his football players walked out of school and were beaten.
related_events: 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer in Holmes County
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge

chapters.title: Saints Junior College and Adult Education
chapters.summary: When the Holmes County superintendent refused Clark's request to implement an adult education program (and the school board told him such a program would only happen if a superintendent requested it), Clark threatened to run for superintendent and was not rehired. Dr. Arenia C. Mallory hired him at Saints Junior College in Lexington, where he ran a six-day-a-week adult-education program reaching three hundred adults including future voter J.P. Love, who later accused Clark of forging signatures unaware that Clark's program had taught Love to read and write.
related_events: Holmes County school board adult-education refusal
Saints Junior College adult-education expansion

chapters.title: 1967 Independent Legislative Campaign
chapters.summary: After the legislature passed a local-and-private act preventing Clark from running for superintendent (later ruled unconstitutional), he ran for the Mississippi House. Holmes County had been redistricted from two seats to one; Clark ran as an independent because Black Mississippians could not be Democratic Party members. He ran against incumbent JP Love (Wilbur Hucks lost the Democratic primary to Love). The Durant vote, organized in part through the residence and community network connected to the Newman political machine, put Clark over after he trailed in early returns. JP Love contested the election; Marian Wright was Clark's lead lawyer.
related_events: 1967 Mississippi legislative election
Voting Rights Act enforcement in Holmes County

chapters.title: Isolation in the Legislature
chapters.summary: Clark was not informed he would be seated until fifteen minutes before swearing-in. As the only Black member among 121 white legislators, the Speaker had a desk moved to the back of the chamber to avoid sitting next to Clark, and for eight years no one sat beside Clark on the front row. He could not get the floor to make motions, prepared exhaustively for education legislation, and after about eight years began advising older white legislators on votes from districts including Vicksburg and Greenville.
related_events: Mississippi House seating of first Black legislator since Reconstruction

chapters.title: Buddie Newman and the Education Chair
chapters.summary: Speaker John R. Junkin grew ill, and Buddie Newman approached Clark in the Capitol needing one more signature to secure Speaker Pro Tempore. Clark signed; Newman dropped to one knee and kissed the pen. When Newman became Speaker after Junkin's death, he made Clark Vice Chair of Education. After Clark gave a 1976-era NAACP Convention keynote in Vicksburg that the newspaper headlined as a "Black Power Speak," Newman called him in but promised the chairmanship would come at the next vacancy and ultimately appointed him over the Vicksburg member's district claim.
related_events: Speaker John R. Junkin's death
Clark's appointment as House Education Committee chair

chapters.title: Education Reform Act of 1982
chapters.summary: Clark and Governor William Winter built an education reform bill; the Rules Committee repeatedly kicked it back to the calendar's bottom, and letters threatening committee members' families circulated from Cleveland, Yazoo City, and Clinton. After Winter took the bill to the public, including the Mississippi Southern Baptists and the Farm Bureau, he called a special session. The Conference Committee was tied up for three weeks; Senator Ellis Bodron's son helped move the conference. Clark accepted a half-percent tax he had publicly opposed to break the impasse. The act restored compulsory school attendance (repealed around the Brown decision) and added early childhood kindergarten.
related_events: 1982 Mississippi special legislative session on education
Mississippi Education Reform Act passage

chapters.title: Congressional Campaigns
chapters.summary: Clark ran twice against Webb Franklin for the U.S. House of Representatives, with the first district stretching from Port Gibson to Webster County. He recounts campaigning alongside John Stennis's nephew and being personal friends with Jim Eastland. The Eddie Carthan controversy in Tchula depressed Holmes County turnout when Clark declined to embrace Carthan publicly. Franklin's campaign slogan "Webb Franklin, he's one of us" Clark identifies as overt racial appeal. He came close in both races; the district was later redrawn into a majority-Black configuration that elected Mike Espy.
related_events: Clark's two U.S. House campaigns against Webb Franklin
Eddie Carthan trial in Tchula
Mississippi 2nd District redistricting

chapters.title: Speaker Pro Tempore and Reflections
chapters.summary: Clark was elected Speaker Pro Tempore in 1992, receiving 17 of 35 Black legislators' votes and 87 percent of the total House vote. He married at thirty-eight, raised his nephew (now a cardiologist in California), and both of his sons became lawyers. Asked the closing question, Clark identifies political parties as the greatest hindrance in Mississippi and warns that without leadership the gains of the civil rights movement could be lost.
related_events: Clark's 1992 election as Speaker Pro Tempore
Growth of Black caucus to 35 members by 2000

loc_video.caption: Robert G. Clark, Jr., oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Pickens, Mississippi, 2013 March 13
